agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A global logistics platform is adding a driver-facing earnings transparency feature that must comply with three distinct legal and labor regimes. The feature displays real-time earnings breakdowns (base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, deductions) to drivers. The jurisdictions differ fundamentally in worker classification, labor law requirements, and institutional approval processes, making this a multi-bounded-context problem with regulatory constraints that override product convenience.","bounded_context":"Driver Compensation Visibility — a read-model over the Payroll/Earnings bounded context, projected into a driver-facing UI. This context does NOT own earnings calculation; it owns the rules for what may be displayed, when, and to whom, subject to jurisdictional constraints.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Base Pay","definition":"The guaranteed compensation component for a work period (hourly wage for employees, per-task or per-period rate for contractors), before bonuses, tips, or deductions."},{"term":"Per-Delivery Bonus","definition":"A variable compensation component paid per completed delivery, governed by platform incentive rules and potentially by collective agreements."},{"term":"Tips","definition":"Gratuities from customers passed through to the driver. Legally protected in most jurisdictions — must never be commingled with or offset against base pay."},{"term":"Deductions","definition":"Amounts subtracted from gross earnings: taxes, social contributions, equipment fees, insurance, penalties. The legality and required disclosure of each deduction type varies by jurisdiction."},{"term":"Earnings Breakdown","definition":"The itemized, driver-visible decomposition of gross and net earnings for a given period, including all components and deductions."},{"term":"Worker Classification","definition":"The legal status of the driver (employee vs. independent contractor), which determines which wage, tax, and transparency laws apply. This is a jurisdictional fact, not a platform choice."},{"term":"Works Council (Betriebsrat)","definition":"A legally mandated employee representative body in Germany with co-determination rights over changes to compensation display systems under BetrVG §87(1)."},{"term":"Wage Transparency","definition":"The legal or policy requirement to make compensation components visible and understandable to the worker. In some jurisdictions this is a hard legal mandate; in others it is a platform choice."},{"term":"Jurisdictional Compensation Policy","definition":"The set of rules governing what earnings components must, may, or must not be displayed in a given legal jurisdiction."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Tips must never be used to offset or reduce base pay in any jurisdiction. They must appear as a separate, clearly labeled line item.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Commingling tips with base pay is illegal in California (Labor Code §351), violates German labor norms, and constitutes fraud against the driver in any jurisdiction. This is a data integrity constraint — the system is corrupt if tips reduce base pay.","testable_assertion":"In the earnings breakdown, the 'tips' line item is always >= 0 and the 'base pay' value is identical whether or not tips exist for that period."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"In California, the full earnings breakdown (base pay, bonuses, tips, and all deductions) must be displayed to the driver. Omitting any component violates state wage transparency law.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"California Labor Code §226 requires itemized wage statements for employees. AB5 classifies these drivers as employees. Failure to display is a legal violation that exposes the platform to statutory penalties per pay period per driver.","testable_assertion":"For any driver whose jurisdiction is 'CA-US', the earnings breakdown includes non-null values for base_pay, per_delivery_bonus, tips, and deductions with itemized deduction categories."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"In Germany, the earnings transparency feature must NOT be deployed to drivers until explicit works council (Betriebsrat) approval is obtained and recorded in the system.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 gives the works council co-determination rights over technical systems that monitor employee performance or behavior. Compensation display systems qualify. Deploying without approval is a violation of German labor law and can result in injunctions.","testable_assertion":"The feature flag for 'earnings_transparency' in jurisdiction 'DE' is gated behind a 'works_council_approval' status that must be 'approved' with a recorded approval date before the feature activates for any German driver."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Deductions must be itemized by category (tax, social contribution, equipment, insurance, penalty, etc.) — never shown as a single lump sum.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Itemization is a strong business policy required in California and best practice elsewhere, but a VP could decide to show aggregated deductions in jurisdictions with no legal itemization requirement.","testable_assertion":"The deductions section of the earnings breakdown contains one or more named categories, each with an individual amount, and the sum of categories equals the total deductions amount."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"In Indonesia, the platform may display earnings breakdowns at its discretion, but must not misrepresent the driver's classification. Drivers must be labeled as independent contractors, not employees.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Misrepresenting worker classification creates legal exposure — it could be used as evidence of an employment relationship, triggering obligations the platform has not accounted for. This is a data integrity issue.","testable_assertion":"For any driver whose jurisdiction is 'ID', the earnings UI does not use the terms 'wage', 'salary', or 'employee'; it uses 'earnings', 'payout', or 'contractor payment'."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Earnings data must reflect real-time or near-real-time state — the displayed breakdown must not lag behind the source payroll system by more than the jurisdictionally defined SLA.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The acceptable lag is a business policy decision. 'Real-time' in the spec likely means low-latency, not literally zero-lag. A VP could adjust the SLA.","testable_assertion":"The timestamp on the displayed earnings breakdown is within the configured SLA threshold of the latest payroll event for that driver."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The earnings breakdown must be scoped to the authenticated driver only. No driver may view another driver's earnings.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Exposing one driver's earnings to another is a privacy violation (GDPR in Germany, CCPA in California) and a data breach. The system is corrupt if this constraint is violated.","testable_assertion":"An API request for earnings with driver_id X authenticated as driver_id Y (where X ≠ Y) returns 403 Forbidden."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver classified as an employee in California","when":"they open the earnings breakdown screen","then":"they see itemized base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and itemized deductions with categories, all reflecting current pay period data"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver in Germany where works council approval has NOT been granted","when":"the platform attempts to enable the earnings transparency feature","then":"the feature remains disabled and the driver sees no earnings breakdown UI"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A driver in Germany where works council approval HAS been granted and recorded","when":"they open the earnings breakdown screen","then":"they see the full earnings breakdown, and the approval record is auditable"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A driver classified as an independent contractor in Indonesia","when":"they open the earnings breakdown screen","then":"they see earnings components using contractor-appropriate language (no 'wage' or 'salary' terms) and the display complies with platform policy"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A driver with tips recorded for the current period","when":"the earnings breakdown is calculated","then":"the base pay amount is unchanged compared to a scenario with zero tips — tips do not offset base pay"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"Any authenticated driver","when":"they request earnings data","then":"only their own earnings are returned; requests for other drivers' data are rejected with 403"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A new jurisdiction is added to the platform","when":"no jurisdictional compensation policy is configured for it","then":"the earnings transparency feature defaults to disabled for that jurisdiction until policy is explicitly configured"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A driver relocates from Indonesia to California mid-pay-period","expected_behavior":"The system must recalculate and re-display earnings under California rules from the effective date of jurisdiction change. Prior Indonesian earnings for that period are displayed under Indonesian rules. The two segments are clearly separated."},{"scenario":"Works council approval in Germany is revoked after feature launch","expected_behavior":"The feature must be immediately disabled for all German drivers. Any cached or pre-rendered earnings screens must not remain accessible. The system must treat revocation as a hard stop, not a graceful degradation."},{"scenario":"A California driver has zero deliveries in a pay period but has deductions","expected_behavior":"The earnings breakdown still displays: base pay (which may be non-zero due to guaranteed minimum hours), per-delivery bonus of $0, tips of $0, and itemized deductions. The screen must not be hidden or show 'no data'."},{"scenario":"Platform adds a new deduction type (e.g., 'uniform fee') not previously categorized","expected_behavior":"The deduction must appear as a named line item, not silently folded into an 'other' category. In California, any new deduction type must be displayed immediately to comply with itemization requirements."},{"scenario":"Tips are negative (customer reversal or chargeback)","expected_behavior":"The tip reversal must be shown as a separate, clearly labeled negative line item — never silently subtracted from base pay or bonus. The driver must understand why their tip total decreased."},{"scenario":"Earnings data is temporarily unavailable due to payroll system outage","expected_behavior":"The UI must show a clear 'data unavailable' state with the last known timestamp, not stale data presented as current. In California, the platform must still meet wage statement delivery deadlines regardless of display system outages."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Non-compliance in California carries penalties of $50-$100 per pay period per employee (Labor Code §226(e)), scaling to millions across a driver fleet. Germany non-compliance can result in injunctions halting operations. Correct implementation avoids these costs and builds driver trust, reducing churn (driver acquisition costs $300-$1,500 per driver in competitive markets).","operational":"Three distinct jurisdictional rulesets require a policy-driven architecture — hardcoding jurisdiction logic creates unsustainable maintenance burden as the platform expands. Works council gating in Germany introduces a human approval dependency into the deployment pipeline that must be modeled explicitly.","user":"Drivers gain trust and autonomy through transparent earnings. In California, this is a legal right. Globally, transparency reduces support tickets ('why was my pay less than expected?') — typically 15-30% of driver support volume relates to pay confusion.","risk":"Primary risks: (1) deploying in Germany without works council approval — legal injunction risk, (2) tip commingling in any jurisdiction — class action exposure, (3) data leakage between drivers — GDPR/CCPA breach with per-incident fines up to €20M or 4% of global revenue. Secondary risk: inconsistent language around worker classification could be used as evidence in reclassification lawsuits.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers (California)","impact":"Gain legally mandated wage transparency; improved trust and reduced pay disputes"},{"group":"Drivers (Germany)","impact":"Feature availability is blocked until works council approves; must not be promised to drivers prematurely"},{"group":"Drivers (Indonesia)","impact":"Receive transparency as a platform benefit, not a legal right; language must reinforce contractor status"},{"group":"Works Council (Germany)","impact":"Must be engaged as a co-decision-maker, not merely notified; failure to involve them is a legal violation"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance Team","impact":"Must define and maintain jurisdictional compensation policies; ongoing obligation as new jurisdictions are added"},{"group":"Finance/Payroll Team","impact":"Source system for earnings data; must guarantee data accuracy and SLA for the display layer"},{"group":"Product/Engineering","impact":"Must build a jurisdiction-aware, policy-driven architecture rather than jurisdiction-specific code paths"},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Expected reduction in pay-related tickets; must be trained on new UI to assist driver inquiries"}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec is underspecified in several critical areas: (1) it does not mention works council requirements for Germany, which is a hard legal blocker — this suggests the spec author may not be aware of this constraint, (2) it gives no guidance on how to handle jurisdiction transitions or multi-jurisdiction drivers, (3) 'real time' is undefined and means different things in different contexts, (4) the spec does not address the language/classification implications for Indonesia. The legal and regulatory requirements are well-established in external law, but the spec itself does not encode them, which creates risk that implementation will miss them.","high_confidence_areas":["Tip protection (BR-1) is universally applicable and well-established in law","California itemized wage statement requirements under Labor Code §226","Privacy constraint on earnings data (BR-7) is standard across all jurisdictions","Works council co-determination requirement in Germany is unambiguous in BetrVG"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact SLA definition for 'real time' earnings display","How the platform handles mid-period jurisdiction changes for drivers","Whether Indonesia deployment is intended at launch or deferred","Whether the works council approval process has already begun or is unplanned"],"assumptions":["AB5 classification applies to all drivers on the platform in California, not just some","The platform has an existing payroll/earnings calculation system that this feature reads from","Works council approval has NOT yet been obtained in Germany","The spec intends 'real time' to mean near-real-time with acceptable latency, not literally zero-lag"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says 'real time' but does not define acceptable latency. In California, wage statements have legal delivery deadlines. What is the SLA for earnings data freshness in each jurisdiction?","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Legal/Compliance","Drivers"],"options":["Define a global SLA (e.g., < 5 minutes lag)","Define per-jurisdiction SLAs based on legal requirements","Use eventual consistency with a 'last updated' timestamp"],"recommendation":"Define per-jurisdiction SLAs. California should have the tightest SLA given legal obligations. Display a 'last updated' timestamp in all jurisdictions as a safety net."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The spec does not mention Germany's works council (Betriebsrat) co-determination requirement. Has the works council been consulted? Has an approval process begun? Without this, the Germany rollout cannot proceed and the feature cannot be promised to German drivers.","affected_stakeholders":["Works Council (Germany)","Legal/Compliance","Drivers (Germany)","Product"],"options":["Block Germany rollout entirely until works council approval is obtained","Begin works council consultation process immediately in parallel with development","Exclude Germany from the initial feature scope"],"recommendation":"Exclude Germany from the initial rollout scope and immediately begin works council consultation. Build the feature with a jurisdiction-gating mechanism so Germany can be enabled once approval is recorded."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec does not address language and terminology differences required by worker classification. California employees should see 'wages' and 'pay'; Indonesia contractors should see 'earnings' and 'payout'. What terminology standard applies in each jurisdiction?","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Drivers","Product"],"options":["Use neutral language ('earnings') globally","Use jurisdiction-specific terminology aligned with worker classification","Let Legal define a terminology map per jurisdiction"],"recommendation":"Legal must define a terminology map per jurisdiction. Using employee-specific language for contractors (or vice versa) creates reclassification risk."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

The feature displays real-time earnings breakdowns (base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, deductions) to drivers.
